coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent turn-off sensor based on sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots in colloidal and film forms for the ultrasensitive detection of carbamate pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Nair, Raji V.
Thomas, Reny Thankam
Mohamed, A.P.
Pillai, Saju
coredata.dc:description: Materials that are highly sensitive, easy-to-use, label-free and ultrafast detection of analyte molecules are in great demand. In the present global scenario, there is immense value to be gained from the development of materials that easily detect harmful substances like pesticides or insecticides in fruits and vegetables, environmental pollutants, or other hazardous chemicals. Herein, we demonstrated fluorescent sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs)-based sensor for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of carbamate pesticides. Green fluorescent S-GQDs with uniform particle size distribution (~5 nm), high crystallinity, monolayer graphene thickness, high quantum yield (27.8%) and high production yield (85%) were synthesised by microwave-assisted sonochemical method using graphene oxide as starting material in five min. KMnO4 is used as oxidising agent and H2SO4 as the precursor for sulphur doping. Sulphur doping is confirmed from XPS and FTIR analysis. Sulphur-doping increases the fluorescence intensity, lifetime and quantum yield of GQDs. This heteroatom doping strategy also results in tuning the optical properties of S-GQD when compared to the undoped system. The green fluorescent S-GQDs in aqueous phase exhibits a detection limit of 0.45 ppb for carbofuran and 1.6 ppb for thiram. Further, S-GQD was incorporated to poly (vinyl alcohol) [PVA] matrix for the preparation of flexible solid-state fluorescent sensing platform. S-GQD imparts fluorescent properties to the polymer film, and thus the film exhibited solid-state fluorescence. PVA/S-GQD flexible film exhibits a LOD of 60 ppb and 210 ppb for carbofuran and thiram, respectively. The selectivity of S-GQD based fluorescent sensor towards carbamate pesticide was investigated. Finally, the developed fluorescent sensor has demonstrated to detect carbofuran in real sample with ppb level sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent probe
Solid-state fluorescent material
Sonochemical method
Pesticide detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs)
Fabrication of poly (vinyl) alcohol/S-doped GQD (PVA/S-GQD) composite film
Preparation of poly (vinyl alcohol)/S-GQD (PVA/S-GQD) composite film
Ultrasensitive and selective detection of pesticides using aqueous S-GQD and PVA/S-GQD composite films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing (a) the methodology adapted for the synthesis of S-GQD by microwave-assisted sonochemical method and (b) the fabrication of S-GQD based fluorescent (PVA/S-GQD) films for the ultrasensitive detection of CF.

TEM images of S-GQDs at different magnifications (a) Scale bar: 50 nm. Inset histogram shows the size distribution of S-GQDs, (b) Scale bar: 20 nm. FFT pattern of the corresponding S-GQD is shown in the inset, (c) HR-TEM image of a single S-GQD shows the lattice parameters 0.340 nm, (d) 0.242 nm, (e) AFM height image of S-GQDs, and (f) the corresponding height profile.
(a) Wide angle X-ray scattering patterns, (b) Raman spectra and (c) FTIR spectra of GO and S-GQDs, XPS narrow scan of, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s and (f) S 2p of S-GQDs.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of GQDs and S-GQDs. Inset shows photographs of GQDs and S-GQDs irradiated upon 365 nm UV illumination. (b) Normalised PL spectra of GQDs and S-GQDs at 350 nm and 380 nm excitation, respectively. (c) PL spectra of S-GQDs by changing excitation wavelengths from 400 nm to 470 nm. (d) Tauc plot of GQDs showing the band gap of 3.05 eV. (e) Tauc plot of S-GQDs showing the band gap of 2.95 eV. (f) CIE diagram showing the colour coordinates of GQDs and S-GQDs.

TEM images of (a) Bare PVA, PVA/S-GQD composite films of (b) 10 wt%, (c) 20 wt%, and (d) 30 wt% S-GQD loading. (e) AFM image of PVA/S-GQD composite containing 20 wt% S-GQD. (f) FTIR spectra of bare PVA and PVA/S-GQD composites.

(a) UV–Vis spectra (b) PL spectra of bare PVA and PVA/S-GQD composites (c) CIE diagram showing the colour coordinates of PVA/S-GQD composites with 5, 10, 20 and 30 wt% S-GQD loading. (d) Digital photographs of bare PVA and PVA/S-GQD films with different wt% S-GQD loading captured under UV light of 365 nm excitation.

PL spectra of aqueous S-GQD with two common pesticides namely, (a) Carbofuran and (b) Thiram (c-d) show the corresponding linear regression plots with regression coefficients (R2) values of 0.986 and 0.989, respectively.

PL spectra of PVA/S-GQD nanocomposite film in the presence of two common pesticides namely, (a) Carbofuran and (b) Thiram (c–d) show the corresponding linear regression plots with regression coefficients (R2) values of 0.974 and 0.997, respectively.
Fluorescence intensity of S-GQD (0.05 mg/mL) with and without various pesticides (1 ppm).

(a) PL spectra of S-GQD with various concentrations (0–50 ppb) of CF spiked real samples (b) Linear regression plot of the fluorescent quenching value (ΔF) vs concentration of CF varying from 0 to 50 ppb with regression coefficient (R2) value of 0.988.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent turn-off sensor based on sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots in colloidal and film forms for the ultrasensitive detection of carbamate pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram 695019, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division
CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST), Thiruvananthapuram 695019, Kerala, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Materials, Materials Science and Technology Division
CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (NIIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Materials that are highly sensitive, easy-to-use, label-free and ultrafast detection of analyte molecules are in great demand. In the present global scenario, there is immense value to be gained from the development of materials that easily detect harmful substances like pesticides or insecticides in fruits and vegetables, environmental pollutants, or other hazardous chemicals. Herein, we demonstrated fluorescent sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs)-based sensor for the ultrasensitive and selective detection of carbamate pesticides. Green fluorescent S-GQDs with uniform particle size distribution (~5 nm), high crystallinity, monolayer graphene thickness, high quantum yield (27.8%) and high production yield (85%) were synthesised by microwave-assisted sonochemical method using graphene oxide as starting material in five min. KMnO4 is used as oxidising agent and H2SO4 as the precursor for sulphur doping. Sulphur doping is confirmed from XPS and FTIR analysis. Sulphur-doping increases the fluorescence intensity, lifetime and quantum yield of GQDs. This heteroatom doping strategy also results in tuning the optical properties of S-GQD when compared to the undoped system. The green fluorescent S-GQDs in aqueous phase exhibits a detection limit of 0.45 ppb for carbofuran and 1.6 ppb for thiram. Further, S-GQD was incorporated to poly (vinyl alcohol) [PVA] matrix for the preparation of flexible solid-state fluorescent sensing platform. S-GQD imparts fluorescent properties to the polymer film, and thus the film exhibited solid-state fluorescence. PVA/S-GQD flexible film exhibits a LOD of 60 ppb and 210 ppb for carbofuran and thiram, respectively. The selectivity of S-GQD based fluorescent sensor towards carbamate pesticide was investigated. Finally, the developed fluorescent sensor has demonstrated to detect carbofuran in real sample with ppb level sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are largely used to curb the growth of weeds and pests for increasing crop productivity, however their high dose and persistent use can cause environmental pollution and have adverse effects on human health that include genotoxicity, carcinogenicity, respiratory and immunological disorders, infertility, irritancy, nerve and bone marrow disorders, endocrine disruptive activity, etc. [1–4]. Because of the strict policy guidelines enforced by various government regulators, the maximum tolerance level of pesticide residues in food crops are defined for monitoring purpose. However, it is quite challenging to detect tolerable levels (typically less than ppm level) of pesticide residues present in crops and their impact on ecosystem [5, 6]. Carbofuran (CF) and Thiram (Tr) are commonly applied in modern agriculture to protect the crops, vegetables, fruits from insect attacks in order to boost crop production. CF is marketed under the trade name, Furadan. Tr is used as a fungicide in the world on a large variety of fruits such as apples, pears, strawberries, tomatoes, grapes, peaches etc. [7]. The excessive application of pesticides in agriculture has resulted in environmental imbalance and evolved immense challenges to food safety. Typically, these harmful pesticide residues are detected through conventional analytical methods such as liquid chromatography, ion trap mass spectrometry, electrochemical techniques, etc. [8–11]. Although these methods exhibit excellent sensitivity, they often involve the usage of sophisticated equipment, laborious sample preparation, residue extraction and require trained labour personnel. Therefore, development of an alternative facile, rapid and practical approaches for the trace-level detection of pesticide residues is of great importance for application in food safety and environment monitoring.

Among all, optical methods based on photoluminescence properties of fluorescent probe appears an ideal approach for the facile and ultra-sensitive detection of pesticide residues [12, 13]. Recently, fluorescent probes like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received great attraction as optical sensor because of its exceptional optical properties, tunable surface functionality, and high production yield [14, 15]. When compared to other graphene derived materials, GQDs exhibit superior properties like aqueous dispersibility, high photostability, biocompatibility, lower toxicity, and good chemical and thermal stabilities [16, 17]. These unique properties of GQDs finds application in the field of bioimaging [18], light emitting diodes [19], photovoltaics [20], photocatalysis [21], chemical sensors [22] and biosensors [23]. Moreover, this metal-free GQD exhibits high surface area and rich in carboxylic groups on its surface and edges, that help them to form a complex with other functional chemicals [24]. However, GQD exhibits lower quantum yield compared to inorganic QDs that are toxic in nature. Elemental doping can further enhance its quantum yield with the introduction of localised states which can alter its electron transfer properties, overcoming the self-quenching problem and thus significantly boost its application in various fields such as energy storage, sensors, catalysis, etc. [25–29]. Thus, high demand exists to develop simple, rapid techniques using cost-effective less toxic GQDs as fluorescent probe for the ultrasensitive detection of these pesticide residues.

Herein, we synthesised S-GQDs from graphene oxide (GO) with KMnO4 as an oxidising agent and H2SO4 as doping source for sulphur by microwave-assisted sonochemical method at 90 °C for 5 min. Further, as-prepared S-GQDs were thoroughly studied for its structural and optical properties. S-GQDs showed comparatively higher quantum yield of 27.8%, production yield of 85.0% and longer lifetime. As-prepared S-GQDs showed high crystallinity, uniform size distribution (~5 nm) and monolayer graphene thickness. The S-GQDs also exhibited a versatile colour shift from blue to green with respect to the undoped GQDs. In the present work, we demonstrated fluorescent sensors-based on S-GQDs in aqueous form as well as in flexible film form for the ultrasensitive detection of carbamate pesticides viz. CF and Tr (Scheme 1 ). In order to prepare a solid-state flexible fluorescent sensing probe, PVA was selected as the matrix polymer and PVA/S-GQDs composites were fabricated by solution casting method. In addition to the role of matrix, the flexible polymer can imparts increased mechanical and chemical stabilities to the composite film [30, 31]. The polymer can limit the agglomeration of GQDs and thereby, maintaining their emission properties [32]. Moreover, due to the flexible nature of sensor probe, minimum amount of analyte is required and on-site sample collection would be possible. The as-synthesised S-GQDs and S-GQD based fluorescent (PVA/S-GQD) films were utilised for ultrasensitive and selective detection of carbamate pesticides with LOD at ppb level. Further, fluorescent probe was applied to detect CF residue in real sample with ppb level sensitivity.

Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was procured from SD Fine-Chemicals Limited. Graphite (<150 µm), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), quinine sulphate, Poly(vinyl) alcohol (Mw-31,000-50,000), carbofuran and thiram were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure deionised water (Milli-Q D3, Merck, Germany) was used in all experiments. Various characterisation techniques used are discussed in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Sulphur-doped graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) were synthesised from GO using H2SO4 as precursor for sulphur and KMnO4 as oxidant. GO was synthesised via Hummers method [33]. Briefly, GO (1 mg/mL) and KMnO4 (1 M) were mixed in 1:1 ratio in 10 mL of H2SO4 (0.1 M) and was then treated in a microwave reactor (SienoUWave 1000.uv.us) for 5 min at 90 °C. To remove the unreacted GO, the mixture was then centrifuged for 10 min at 3000 rpm. The supernatant solution after centrifugation was filtered using a 0.45 µm PTFE membrane and the obtained filtrate was then dialysed using 1 kDa dialysis membrane. Pure solid S-GQDs were obtained upon evaporation of solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of poly (vinyl) alcohol/S-doped GQD (PVA/S-GQD) composite film
ce:section.ce:para: PVA/S-GQD composite films were fabricated using PVA pellets and varying amounts of S-GQDs (Table S1) were mixed in 20 mL water by stirring at 70 °C for 4 h. To ensure good dispersion, GQDs were dispersed by bath sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, 5 mL of PVA/S-GQD mixture was taken in a petri dish and dried in vacuum for 24 h at RT in desiccator. PVA/S-GQD nanocomposites containing different weight percentages of S-GQDs (1, 5, 10, 20 and 30 wt%) were prepared.

The sensitive detection of a set of pesticides were done using aqueous solution of S-GQD and its composite film form (PVA/S-GQD) at room temperature. For the detection, two common carbamate pesticides carbofuran (CF) and thiram (Tr) were selected. To study the sensitivity towards these pesticides, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S-GQDs (0.05 mg/mL) as well as PVA/S-GQD composite films (5 wt%) in the presence and absence of these pesticides were obtained with 380 nm excitation. For aqueous form detection, a series of concentrations of CF (0 ppb-225 ppb), and Tr (0 ppb-800 ppb) were freshly prepared and mixed with S-GQDs (0.05 mg/mL). For the study using PVA/S-GQD composite film (5 wt%), the films were immersed in pesticide solutions (CF and Tr) at concentrations ranging from 0-1.5 ppm for 30 min and dried at RT. The quenching effects of these pesticides on the fluorescence of both S-GQD and PVA/S-GQD films were analysed by recording the PL spectra. For selectivity study, a set of pesticides such as imidacloprid, acetamiprid, metalaxyl and carbofuran at a concentration of 1ppm were also analysed. The PL spectra of S-GQD with these pesticides were obtained with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

To test the efficacy of S-GQD-based fluorescent sensors in practical applications, real samples (apple fruit) purchased from local market was tested. The pre-treatment processing of the sample was done as described in the literature [34]. Firstly, the fruit sample was made into a free-flowing puree and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to remove solid contents. Later, the obtained supernatant solution was then filtered using 0.22 µm membrane filter. Various concentrations (0–50 ppb) of carbofuran (CF) were spiked to this filtered solution and mixed with GQDs (0.05 mg/mL) for further analysis. To study the detection efficiency of our sensor towards CF, we carried out the recovery experiments for the CF spiked samples at three different concentrations (1 ppb, 15 ppb, 100 ppb).

S-GQDs were synthesised using H2SO4 as the doping source within short span of time. The structural and morphological analysis of S-GQDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 1 (a–b) showed representative TEM images of S-GQDs having moderately uniform shape and size distribution (~5 nm). The histogram (inset of Fig. 1a) showed that S-GQDs have an average size of 5 nm. Fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of the corresponding TEM image is given in the inset of Fig. 1b. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 1c and d) obtained from a single S-GQD indicated two types of lattice parameter which corresponds to the lattice spacing of d 1002 (0.340 nm) and d 1120 (0.242 nm) planes. The AFM height image (Fig. 1e) showed typical morphology of S-GQD and an average height of 400 pm–800 pm (section profile, Fig. 1f) which indicates highly exfoliated monolayer graphene quantum dots.

Wide angle X-ray scattering spectra of GO sheets and S-GQDs are depicted in Fig. 2 a. In this case, GO revealed a characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ of 10.4° (002) while S-GQDs exhibited less intense, broad peak of (002) plane at 2θ of 25.4° indicative of S-GQDs of small size with a very few layered graphene. Further, Raman spectroscopy was applied to investigate the degree of structural disorder in graphene-based materials [35, 36]. The Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) of S-GQDs exhibited two characteristic peaks centred at 1368 and 1647 cm−1 correspond to the D-band and G-band, respectively. The D-band is usually observed due to the defects in the sp2hybridised carbon whereas G-band refers to in-plane vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms and first order scattering of E2g stretching vibration mode of sp2 carbon of graphitic domains [37, 38]. When compared to undoped GQD, the Raman spectrum of S-GQD showed slight shift in peaks. The D-band was shifted by 11 cm−1 and the G-band by 69 cm−1. Typically, the intensity ratio of D-band (ID) to G-band (IG), [ID/IG] was calculated to understand the structural disorderness in graphene materials [39]. The ID/IG ratio for the S-GQD was estimated to be 1.38 which is higher than that of undoped GQDs. [40] Additionally, similar to other doped graphitic materials, the shift in G-band arises because of the structural distortions in S-GQDs caused by difference in C-C and C-S bond length. Therefore, the augmented ID/IG value and the corresponding shift of the bands of S-GQDs indicated successful doping of sulphur.

The surface functionalities of GO and S-GQDs were studied using FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 2c) revealed that S-GQDs exhibited characteristic peaks of -COOH group (1370 cm−1), -CO group (1710 cm−1) and a broad peak of O-H bending vibrations at 3340 cm−1. The chemical state of sulphur is revealed from the peaks at 1096 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of S=O) and 635 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of C-S). Peak at 1600 cm−1 is attributed to vibrations of benzene ring C=C bonds and that at 1258 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of C-O-C in epoxides. The remarkable water dispersibility with high stability of S-GQD is ascribed due to the presence of –COOH, –OH and –CO groups in S-GQDs.

Furthermore, XPS analysis was performed to study the extent of sulphur doping in S-GQDs. Survey spectrum (Fig. S1) of S-GQDs clearly showed the existence of C, O and S elements with 58.5%, 37.3% and 3.3% atomic percentages, respectively (Table S2). Fig. 2(d–f) represents the narrow scan of C 1s, O 1s and S 2p in S-GQDs. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of S-GQDs (Fig. 2d) revealed the presence of C=C (284.5 eV), C=O (288 eV) and C-S (286.8 eV) bonds which is inline with FTIR results. Among all deconvoluted peaks, C=C component shows a higher percentage value which means that S-GQDs possess abundant graphitic structure. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) of S-GQDs was deconvoluted to three peaks: 530.2 eV, 531.5 eV and 529.5 eV which corresponds to -COOH, -OH and C-O, respectively. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2f) inferred the presence of C-S bonds in S-GQDs. Peaks at 167.0 eV and 168.5 eV in the S 2p narrow scan of S-GQDs confirmed the presence of oxide sulphur and C-S bond in S-GQDs.

The optical properties of the green S-GQDs were analysed using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra. The UV–Vis spectra of S-GQDs (Fig. 3 a) showed a broad peak at 380 nm and a sharp peak below 300 nm, which is due to the n–π* transition and π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, respectively. Compared to UV–Vis spectra of neat GQDs, the S-GQDs showed a slight red shift from 350 nm to 380 nm. The inset of Fig. 3a depicts digital photographs of blue coloured GQD and green coloured S-GQD irradiated upon 365 nm UV lamp. The PL spectra of GQDs and S-GQDs showed the characteristic emission maxima at 430 nm and 500 nm that corresponds to blue and green emissions, respectively (Fig. 3b). Thus the heteroatom doping strategy results in tuning the optical properties of S-GQD when compared to the undoped GQD [41]. In addition, S-GQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission and as the excitation is altered from 400 to 470 nm, a red shift is noted with a significant decrease in PL intensity as shown in Fig. 3c. The shift in emission colours of GQDs and S-GQDs were further confirmed by band gap calculation using Tauc plot. Fig. 3d and e represent Tauc plots of GQDs and S-GQDs, respectively. In comparison with the undoped GQDs, S-GQDs showed a reduction in band gap from 3.05 eV to 2.95 eV which suggests the red shift in PL spectra of S-GQD [42]. Fig. 3f depicts the respective coordinates in the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) diagram of GQDs and S-GQDs which confirms the shift in emission spectra. Quinine sulphate in 0.05 M sulphuric acid as reference was used to calculate Quantum yield (QY). In comparison with GQDs which showed QY of 23.7% [43], S-GQDs showed an relatively higher QY of 27.8% (Table S3). As carbon and sulphur atoms have similar electronegativity, sulphur atoms might replace some carbon atoms and decrease the aromatic-rich carbon-domains leading to its improved fluorescence quantum yield. [44] The PL lifetime of S-GQDs obtained by time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) under 370 nm excitation. The PL lifetime of S-GQDs obtained was 3.3 ns and has two components: 1.82 ns (ca. 38.85%) and 6.78 ns (ca. 61.15%) which is longer than undoped GQDs (2.5 ns) (Fig. S2). Doping in the system introduces localised states that can trap photo-excited electrons and thus hence enhances its fluorescence lifetime [13, 45]. The prolonged lifetime could be beneficial for its application as a fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of poly (vinyl alcohol)/S-GQD (PVA/S-GQD) composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Poly (vinyl alcohol)/S-GQD composite films containing different weight percentages (1, 5, 10, 20, and 30 wt%) of S-GQDs were fabricated by mixing corresponding amounts of PVA pellet and S-GQDs in water and stirring at 70 °C for 4 h. Later, 5 mL of PVA/S-GQD dispersion was solution casted and dried at ambient temperature. TEM images show a uniform distribution of S-GQDs in PVA film (Fig. 4 a–d). Fig. 4a shows TEM image of bare PVA film and Fig. 4(b–d) show TEM images of different weight percentages of S-GQD (10, 20 and 30 wt%) loading in PVA matrix. The images with lower loading of S-GQDs exhibited higher dispersibility in PVA matrix without any additional chemical treatments as it possesses several surface functionalities. This would be a remarkable advantage of S-GQDs over other semiconductor QDs which typically require surface treatments to avoid agglomeration [30]. Additionally, the AFM image (Fig. 4e) shows well-dispersed S-GQDs in the PVA matrix.

To understand the nature chemical functionalities between the PVA matrix and S-GQDs, FTIR analysis was carried out. The FTIR spectra of bare PVA and PVA/S-GQD films with various S-GQD loadings are shown in Fig. 4f. In the case of bare PVA, a broad peak at 3296 cm−1appeared because of the symmetrical stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. In general, the hydroxyl stretching vibration is prone to the association or disassociation of hydrogen bonding, that will decrease the stretching frequency of O-H bonds [46]. Fig. 4f shows that on increasing the amount of S-GQD loading, there is a shift in -OH stretching vibration to lower wavenumber in the PVA/S-GQDs composite films (from 3296 cm−1 of bare PVA to 3261 cm−1 of PVA/S-GQD 30 wt%). Thus, the FTIR analysis indicates the formation of hydrogen bonding in PVA/S-GQDs films.

The optical properties of PVA/S-GQD composite films were investigated using UV–Visible and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 5 a displays UV–Vis spectra of bare PVA and PVA/S-GQD films containing various weight percentages of S-GQDs (1, 5, 10, 20 and 30 wt%). It is obvious that the broad absorption peak at 380 nm and a strong peak below 300 nm were observed, that correspond to the n–π* transition and π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, respectively. The PL spectra of PVA/S-GQD films look analogous to that of S-GQD whereas the intensity changes by varying S-GQD loading (Fig. 5b). The incorporation of S-GQD in PVA matrix imparts luminescence properties to PVA/S-GQD composite films. Fig. 5c display the respective colour coordinates in the CIE diagram of PVA/S-GQD composite films. As PVA/S-GQD films exhibited broad PL spectrum covering the entire visible range, these materials may find application in white LED as luminophores [46]. The luminescence behaviour of the composite films was visualised by capturing its photographs under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasensitive and selective detection of pesticides using aqueous S-GQD and PVA/S-GQD composite films
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs have been used as fluorescent sensors because of its excellent optical properties and other characteristics of high photostability and size tunability. Here, we employed as-prepared S-GQDs both in aqueous form and solid state (PVA/S-GQD composites) for the detection of two carbamate pesticides (CF and Tr) which are extensively used in the agricultural sector to increase crop production. The influence of CF and Tr on the fluorescent intensity of S-GQD in the aqueous phase and composite film were studied by recording its PL spectra in the presence and absence of these pesticides. Experiments were conducted at varying concentrations of CF and Tr, in both solution phase and composite film. It was observed that the fluorescence quenching by these pesticides were concentration dependent. Upon increasing the pesticides concentration, the fluorescence intensity of aqueous S-GQD (Fig. 6 a–b) and PVA/S-GQD composites (Fig. 7 a–b) decreases gradually. It is noteworthy that in solution phase, S-GQD shows significant quenching upon increasing the concentration of pesticides with a LOD of 0.45 ppb for CF, 1.6 ppb for Tr. Fig. 6(c–d) show the corresponding linear regression plots of S-GQD with CF, and Tr. Further, PVA/S-GQD composite film as solid state fluorescent sensor was also tested for the detection of these pesticides and it was found that LOD was 60 ppb and 210 ppb for CF, and Tr, respectively. Fig. 7(c–d) shows the corresponding linear regression plots of PVA/S-GQD composite films with CF, and Tr. The mechanism of fluorescent quenching in both aqueous S-GQD and PVA/S-GQD composites by these pesticides is due to the complexation followed by the photo-induced electron transfer mechanism. The oxygen atoms of surface carboxyl groups and sulphur atoms of sulphonic groups of S-GQD, have strong electronegativity and can form complex with carbamate groups of these pesticides through hydrogen bonding. An electron from this complex jumps from lower energy ground state to higher energy excited state with the absorption of photons, and then returns to ground state with the release of heat to surroundings, and the quenching of S-GQDs occur [47]. Thus, based on the quenching responses of S-GQD, both in aqueous solution and solid state PVA/S-GQD film by these pesticides, a facile fluorescence sensor to detect some commonly used toxic carbamate pesticides were demonstrated with fast response and LOD in ppb level. We achieved a remarkable LOD of the pesticides such as carbofuran (0.45 ppb) and thiram (1.6 ppb) in parts per billion (ppb) level. A comparison of different carbofuran detection methods have been tabulated in Table S5. Thus, the present method has a very low LOD of 0.45 ppb (2.03 × 10−12 M) with respect to many other methods.

Further, the selectivity of S-GQD based fluorescent sensor was demonstrated using a set of pesticides such as imidacloprid, acetamiprid, metalaxyl and carbofuran. Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence quenching behaviour of S-GQD with and without these pesticides. It was observed that addition of CF pesticide showed significant quenching with the fluorescence change ratio [(I0-I)/I0] of 84%. While the addition of other control pesticides under identical conditions resulted in minor or no significant change with a relative fluorescence change ratio of 1–18% when compared to blank. These results confirmed the selectivity of S-GQD based sensor towards CF pesticide.

In a challenging experiment, we tested the viability of employing S-GQD -based fluorescent sensor in real sample analysis for detection of CF pesticide in apple fruit. The PL spectra of S-GQD with various concentrations of CF spiked apple samples are shown in Fig. 9 a. From the Fig. 9a, it is obvious that S-GQD showed significant quenching upon increasing the concentration of CF with a LOD of 0.5 ppb. The fluorescent quenching value (ΔF) showed a linear increase with increase in concentration of CF from 0 to 10 ppb (Fig. 9b). The detection efficiency of the pesticide was studied by performing the recovery experiments for the CF spiked samples at three different concentrations and is shown in Table S4 and exhibited 93–96% recovery.

In summary, a facile S-GQD-based fluorescence sensor was demonstrated for selective and ultrasensitive detection of commonly used carbamate pesticides. Herein, S-GQDs with stable green fluorescence, higher quantum yield and production yield was synthesised from graphene oxide within 5 min. Successful doping of sulphur atom in S-GQD was confirmed from XPS and FTIR spectroscopy. As part of realisation of a simple flexible solid-state fluorescent sensor, PVA/S-GQD composite films were fabricated. Both aqueous S-GQD colloid and PVA/S-GQD flexible films were demonstrated as excellent fluorescent sensors for the ultrasensitive detection of pesticides with a remarkable LOD at ppb level for CF (0.45 ppb) and Tr (1.6 ppb). The selectivity of S-GQD-based fluorescent probe towards carbofuran pesticide was studied. Additionally, the S-GQD-based fluorescent probe was successfully employed to detect CF pesticide in real samples with ppb level sensitivity. Thus, our approach would be suitable for the cost-effective synthesis of relatively non-toxic quantum dots with high production yield which can be applied as viable fluorescent sensors to detect pesticide residues at trace-level for food safety and environment monitoring.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Mr. Kiran Mohan and Mr. Vishnu for TEM image and AFM image acquisitions, respectively. Dr. Bhoje Gowd E. and Dr. Karunakaran V. are acknowledged for helping in WAXS and TCSPC measurements, respectively. The authors acknowledge Dr. Parvathy R. Chandran (CSIR-NIIST) for fruitful discussions. R.V.N. is thankful for CSIR fellowship and R.T.T. thanks KSCSTE for postdoctoral fellowship (KSCSTE/309/2018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104971.
